metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update `src/rebar/_cli/help/import.txt` (and regenerate `docs/cli-reference.md` via `scripts/gen_cli_reference.py`) to reflect that idempotent skip-by-source_id and deferred-push are already implemented, removing the 'added in a follow-up' caveat, so the `--help` output matches both the code and the plan's asserted behavior.
evidence: import.txt line 15-17: 'Note: this importer always creates (re-running duplicates). Idempotent skip-by-source_id and deferred-push performance are added in a follow-up.'
import_ndjson.py lines 90-105 (_scan_existing_source_ids) and lines 200-231 (seen_source skip logic) confirm idempotency IS implemented.
import_ndjson.py lines 212-213 and 378-381 confirm push deferral IS implemented.
Plan Remediation: 'is idempotent by source_id … re-running the same fallback payload through rebar import later is then a no-op, not a duplicate.'
AC7: 'states it accepts NDJSON … assigns fresh local ids/HLC timestamps … is idempotent by source_id'
MISMATCH: CLI --help surface contradicts the plan's asserted behavior and the actual code.
findings.finding: The pinned help text for `rebar import` (`src/rebar/_cli/help/import.txt` lines 15-17) explicitly states 'this importer always creates (re-running duplicates)' and 'Idempotent skip-by-source_id … added in a follow-up,' but the actual implementation (`src/rebar/_io/import_ndjson.py`, `_scan_existing_source_ids` + pass-1 loop) IS already idempotent by source_id and DOES defer push. The plan's guidance and AC7 assert idempotency as a live, load-bearing safety property of the exceptional import path. An operator who runs `rebar import --help` before invoking the sanctioned import path will read that re-runs duplicate tickets — directly contradicting the guidance — potentially causing them to either distrust the plan or avoid re-runs that are actually safe.
findings.location: Remediation section / AC7 (exceptional import mechanism); src/rebar/_cli/help/import.txt lines 15-17
scenarios: An operator follows the guide, runs `rebar import --help` before invoking the exceptional import, reads 'always creates (re-running duplicates)', and either distrusts the idempotency claim in the guidance or mistakenly fears a re-run will create duplicates.

A ChatGPT session with GitHub connector reads the help text via repo file access and uses the stale warning to override the guidance, incorrectly warning the operator that idempotency is not available.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace lines 14-17 of `src/rebar/_cli/help/import.txt` with: 'Idempotent by source_id: a scan of the target at start skips any record whose source_id already exists, so re-runs and resume-after-crash never duplicate. Push is deferred for the duration (one final push). Run summary (created, skipped, links, comments, warnings) is printed to stderr.' Then run `python scripts/gen_cli_reference.py` to regenerate the CLI reference.

findings.finding: The plan relies on `rebar import`'s idempotent-by-source_id guarantee as a safety property for the exceptional import path (AC7, Remediation 'Exceptional import mechanism'), directing operators to re-run the same NDJSON payload without fear of duplication. However, the CLI's own `--help` text (docs/cli-reference.md lines 220-222) explicitly states the opposite: 'Note: this importer always creates (re-running duplicates). Idempotent skip-by-source_id and deferred-push performance are added in a follow-up.' The code itself IS idempotent (src/rebar/_io/import_ndjson.py implements _scan_existing_source_ids and skips already-imported source_ids), but a ChatGPT agent or operator consulting `rebar import --help` before execution will read a direct contradiction of the plan's stated guarantee, undermining the safety property the plan depends on.
findings.location: Remediation — 'Exceptional import mechanism' / AC7 / docs/cli-reference.md lines 220-222 / src/rebar/_io/import_ndjson.py
findings.suggested_fix: Update the CLI help text for `rebar import` to read: '--dry-run  Report how many tickets would be created/skipped; write nothing\nIdempotent by source_id: a re-run skips already-imported tickets (source_id already present in the target). Run summary (counts, warnings) is printed to stderr.' This keeps the help text accurate and removes the contradiction.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the fallback payload required-field list with `export.schema.json`'s composed schema: either add `priority` (integer 0-4) and `tags` (empty array `[]` as safe defaults) to the fallback spec, or explicitly note that `rebar import` only enforces `ticket_id`/`ticket_type` and the schema-conformance claim should be scoped accordingly.
evidence: src/rebar/schemas/export.schema.json line 6: 'allOf': [{'$ref': 'ticket_state.schema.json'}]
src/rebar/schemas/ticket_state.schema.json line 8: 'required': ['ticket_id', 'ticket_type', 'title', 'status', 'priority', 'tags']

Plan states: 'the fallback payload MUST conform to src/rebar/schemas/export.schema.json's actual required array — schema_version, ticket_id, ticket_type, title, status' — missing priority and tags from the composed required set
AC8 invariant 5: 'The guide's fallback-payload field list matches src/rebar/schemas/export.schema.json's actual required array (schema_version, ticket_id, ticket_type, title, status)' — same omission

Note: rebar import itself only checks ticket_id/ticket_type (not schema-validates), so this is a schema-conformance gap rather than a hard import failure, but the plan claims the payload 'MUST conform to' the schema
findings.finding: The fallback payload schema specification (and AC8 test invariant 5) only lists `export.schema.json`'s own `required` array, but that schema composes `ticket_state.schema.json` via `allOf`, which adds `priority` and `tags` to the effective required set under JSON Schema 2020-12 — making a payload that omits those fields technically invalid against the composed schema.
findings.location: Remediation — Fallback payload schema paragraph; AC8 invariant 5
scenarios: The guide instructs ChatGPT to omit `priority` and `tags`; a maintainer who runs a JSON Schema validator against the payload before importing it sees validation errors

AC8 invariant 5's test asserts the guide lists exactly [schema_version, ticket_id, ticket_type, title, status] and passes — but a schema-aware reviewer finds the composed-schema required set is larger
findings.suggested_fix: Add `priority: 2` (default mid-range) and `tags: []` to the fallback payload field list, and update AC8 invariant 5's expected required array to include them, or qualify the claim as 'export.schema.json's own required array (not the composed allOf set)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the dry-run shortfall-detection rule to specify that the comparison baseline is the count of **valid JSON records** yielded by parsing (not the raw NDJSON line count), since `_iter_records` silently discards blank lines and warns-skips unparseable lines before the dry-run loop — and that a shortfall therefore indicates missing-`ticket_id`/`ticket_type` records OR unparseable lines, not exclusively the former.
evidence: src/rebar/_io/import_ndjson.py lines 67-73: _iter_records skips blank lines (if not line: continue) and warns-and-skips JSON decode errors before yielding any record
src/rebar/_io/import_ndjson.py lines 133: 'records = list(_iter_records(source))' — the records list fed to both dry-run and live-run has already discarded blank/unparseable lines
Plan text: 'any shortfall is exactly the count of malformed (missing ticket_id/ticket_type) records, since those are the only category dry-run drops without incrementing either counter'

Blank lines and unparseable JSON lines are dropped by _iter_records with no counter increment and (in dry-run) no warning — they also reduce 'total records' relative to 'total NDJSON lines', making the plan's 'EXACTLY' claim wrong if the operator counts raw lines
findings.finding: The dry-run shortfall-detection rule ('any shortfall [in would_create + skipped vs. total NDJSON line/record count] is EXACTLY the count of malformed records') is incorrect because `_iter_records` silently skips blank lines and warns-and-skips JSON-unparseable lines before they reach the dry-run counter loop — so both blank lines and unparseable lines also produce a shortfall, not only missing-ticket_id/ticket_type records.
findings.location: Remediation — Exceptional import mechanism paragraph; AC7
scenarios: Operator counts raw NDJSON line count (including blank separator lines), compares to would_create + skipped, sees a shortfall of 3 — but 2 of those are blank lines and only 1 is a malformed record; they incorrectly diagnose 3 malformed records and re-examine valid records
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'compare would_create + skipped against the total NDJSON line/record count' with 'compare would_create + skipped against the count of non-blank, valid-JSON lines (records _iter_records successfully parsed); a shortfall may indicate either missing ticket_id/ticket_type fields or JSON-parse errors, not exclusively the former — check stderr warnings to distinguish the two categories.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the full lifecycle of fallback NDJSON payloads in the guidance: (a) where the payload is handed off (e.g., quoted in the conversation response, written to a named file if the filesystem is available), (b) how operators track which payloads have been imported (e.g., recommend tagging the UUID4 in a parent ticket comment or maintaining a local import log), and (c) what a subsequent session should do before generating a new fallback payload for the same work item (e.g., search the tracker by title/description to detect a ticket already created from a prior session's payload).
evidence: Plan states the agent 'produces a reviewable ticket payload' but does not specify the storage medium (conversation output, a file, a GitHub comment, etc.)

Plan states rebar import is idempotent by source_id — this handles re-import of the SAME payload, but if two sessions independently generate two different UUID4s for the same conceptual ticket, both will import as separate tickets

ABSENCE: no plan element enumerates consumers of fallback payloads (who runs rebar import?), no retirement/cleanup path (how does a maintainer know which payloads have already been imported?), no deduplication step before import
findings.finding: The lifecycle of agent-generated fallback NDJSON payloads is underspecified: the plan defines how to CREATE the payload (agent generates UUID4, produces NDJSON) but does not specify where it is stored between generation and operator import, how multiple sessions targeting the same conceptual work item are deduplicated before import, or how consumed/imported payloads are retired so they are not imported again by a later session.
findings.location: Remediation 'Fallback payload schema' section / AC6
scenarios: Two ChatGPT sessions both determine native creation is impossible and generate separate UUID4 fallback payloads for the same feature; a maintainer imports both, creating duplicate tickets
A fallback payload is generated, later the environment recovers and the agent creates the ticket natively; the old NDJSON is later imported by a different operator, creating a duplicate
Payloads accumulate in conversation history with no tracking of which have been imported

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that the shortfall-detection comparison should be `would_create + skipped == len(records)` (number of parsed non-blank, valid-JSON NDJSON objects, NOT raw line count), and note that JSON-parse-error lines are additionally dropped by `_iter_records` with a logger warning (not surfaced in `warnings[]`), so the raw-line-count comparison is not reliable.
evidence: Plan text: 'any shortfall is exactly the count of malformed (missing ticket_id/ticket_type) records, since those are the only category dry-run drops without incrementing either counter'

src/rebar/_io/import_ndjson.py _iter_records lines 66-73: blank lines (`if not line: continue`) and JSON-parse-error lines (`except json.JSONDecodeError: logger.warning(...); continue`) are silently skipped without incrementing any counter

In dry-run mode `records = list(_iter_records(source))` is called first (line 133), so blank/parse-error lines are dropped BEFORE the dry-run loop, meaning the `records` list the dry-run iterates already excludes them — the total-lines comparison should be against `len(records)` (parsed record objects), NOT raw NDJSON line count
However, the plan says 'comparing `would_create + skipped` against the total NDJSON line/record count', which is ambiguous between raw line count (wrong) and parsed-record count (correct)
findings.finding: The plan's `would_create + skipped == total NDJSON line/record count` shortfall-detection rule asserts that 'any shortfall is EXACTLY the count of malformed (missing ticket_id/ticket_type) records,' but `_iter_records` also silently drops blank lines and JSON-parse-error lines without incrementing any counter — so the shortfall can exceed the actual count of missing-field records, causing the operator to misdiagnose the payload.
findings.location: Remediation section – 'Exceptional import mechanism' paragraph; specifically the sentence: 'any shortfall is exactly the count of malformed (missing ticket_id/ticket_type) records, since those are the only category dry-run drops without incrementing either counter'
scenarios: An NDJSON fallback payload file with trailing blank lines or a comment-like blank separator will show a shortfall that is NOT caused by malformed records, leading an operator to hunt for a missing ticket_id/ticket_type field that does not exist.

An NDJSON file with a JSON-parse-error line (e.g. a typo) will show an inflated shortfall without explanation, since the parse error is only logged to the Python logger (not surfaced in warnings[] or counters).
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'comparing `would_create + skipped` against the total NDJSON line/record count' with: 'comparing `would_create + skipped` against the number of successfully-parsed NDJSON records (i.e., `len(list(_iter_records(file)))` or `wc -l` minus blank lines) — note that `_iter_records` also silently drops blank lines and JSON-parse-error lines (the latter only to the Python logger, not to `warnings[]`), so a raw line-count comparison can over-report the shortfall.'

findings.finding: The plan's dry-run malformed-record detection advice states 'any shortfall [of `would_create + skipped` vs. the total NDJSON line/record count] is exactly the count of malformed records.' The word 'line' is ambiguous: `import_ndjson.py`'s `_iter_records()` skips blank lines and unparseable lines before building `records`, so `len(records)` (parsed dict count) — not the physical line count — is the denominator that makes the arithmetic exact. If an implementor counts physical newlines, the comparison gives a false positive whenever the NDJSON file has trailing newlines or blank separators.
findings.location: Remediation section — 'Exceptional import mechanism' subsection; AC7
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'total NDJSON line/record count' with 'total parsed-record count (non-blank, valid-JSON lines)' and note that a trailing newline or blank separator does not add to the parsed-record count.

findings.finding: The dry-run shortfall-detection instruction — 'any shortfall [between would_create + skipped and the total NDJSON line/record count] is exactly the count of malformed records' — is ambiguous about what counts as a 'record' in 'total record count'. Tool inspection of `import_ndjson.py:_iter_records` (lines 54-75) shows that blank lines are silently skipped (`if not line: continue`) and unparseable JSON lines are warn-and-skipped BEFORE the ticket_id/ticket_type check. These pre-filter drops are NOT included in `would_create` OR `skipped`, yet the plan implies the shortfall equation accounts only for missing-ticket_id/ticket_type records. An operator counting raw NDJSON lines (including blanks and malformed JSON) will get a wrong shortfall count.
findings.location: Remediation → Exceptional import mechanism
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'total NDJSON line/record count' with 'count of successfully JSON-parsed non-blank records (blank lines and JSON-parse failures are upstream drops visible only in warnings, not in would_create or skipped)'. Direct operators to check the warnings list for JSON-parse failures separately.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Qualify the Evidence section's warning-behavior statement by scoping it explicitly to live-run mode (e.g., 'in live-run mode, missing-field records emit a warning … in dry-run mode, no warning is emitted') so it no longer contradicts the Remediation's dry-run description.
evidence: Evidence: 'a missing-`ticket_id`/`ticket_type` record is warned about but does not increment `skipped`; it is invisible to the return summary's counters and detectable only via the `warnings` list'

Remediation (Exceptional import mechanism): 'in dry-run mode (`src/rebar/_io/import_ndjson.py` ~lines 144-161), a record missing `ticket_id`/`ticket_type` hits a bare `continue` with **no counter increment and no warning at all**'
The Evidence section does not label its warning-behavior description as applying to live-run mode only, leaving ambiguity about whether warnings are produced or suppressed.
findings.finding: The Evidence section makes a general, mode-unqualified claim that missing `ticket_id`/`ticket_type` records are 'warned about … and detectable only via the `warnings` list,' while the Remediation section states that in `--dry-run` mode specifically these same records produce 'no warning at all.' An implementer reading both sections receives contradictory signals about whether to document that warnings are emitted for malformed records.
findings.location: Evidence section (lines describing import_ndjson.py behavior) vs. Remediation section 'Exceptional import mechanism' paragraph
scenarios: An implementer who reads the Evidence section before the Remediation writes guidance saying malformed records produce a warning; a second implementer who reads both sections in full is uncertain which behavior to document.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Evidence bullet describing import_ndjson.py behavior, change 'a missing-`ticket_id`/`ticket_type` record is warned about … detectable only via the `warnings` list' to 'in live-run mode, a missing-`ticket_id`/`ticket_type` record emits a warning … detectable only via the `warnings` list; in `--dry-run` mode, the same malformed record is silently dropped with no warning and no counter increment.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC7's proving command (or AC8's sub-invariants) to check for the `would_create + skipped` equality pattern that detects malformed records — e.g., add `grep -qi 'would_create' docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md` — so that the guide's coverage of the detection heuristic is at minimum keyword-verified.
evidence: AC7 states the guide must 'correctly describe missing-ticket_id/ticket_type records as silently skipped with NO warning and NO counter increment in --dry-run mode specifically'
AC7 also states it must 'recommend detecting malformed records via a would_create + skipped == total record count comparison'
The three grep commands check only keyword presence, not correctness of the described behavior
A guide saying 'in dry-run mode, skipped is incremented for malformed records' would still pass all three greps
findings.finding: AC7's proving commands verify only that the keywords 'rebar import', 'rebar validate|rebar fsck', and 'dry-run' appear in the guide, but do NOT verify the guide correctly describes the dry-run behavior — specifically that malformed records (missing `ticket_id`/`ticket_type`) produce NO warning and NO counter increment, and that the correct detection method is `would_create + skipped == total record count`. A guide that mentions 'dry-run' but describes the wrong counter behavior (e.g., 'skipped is incremented') would pass the grep while contradicting the specified behavior.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria – AC7 proving command: `grep -qi 'rebar import' docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md && grep -qi 'rebar validate\|rebar fsck' docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md && grep -qi 'dry-run' docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md`
scenarios: An executor writes accurate-sounding but incorrect dry-run guidance; all three greps pass; the guide silently gives maintainers wrong operational advice that causes undetected record loss during import.
findings.suggested_fix:
Append to AC7's verify line:
`&& grep -qi 'would_create' docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md`
This anchors the one keyword that is unique to the recommended detection heuristic (`would_create + skipped == total`), making it infeasible to pass the check without mentioning the correct comparison.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the closed `ticket_type` enum vocabulary (`bug`, `epic`, `story`, `task`, `session_log`, `code_review`, `identity` — from `src/rebar/schemas/common.schema.json#/$defs/ticket_type`) to the fallback payload field specification so an executor does not need to infer or invent valid values.
evidence: Plan text: 'ticket_type/title/description from the ticket content' — the vocabulary 'from the ticket content' implies any string, but the actual enum is a closed set in src/rebar/schemas/common.schema.json $defs.ticket_type
src/rebar/schemas/common.schema.json line 11-14 defines the enum: ["bug", "epic", "story", "task", "session_log", "code_review", "identity"]

A ChatGPT session constructing a fallback payload needs to know which values are legal; without the enum, it may invent a value (e.g. 'feature') that passes the plan's stated checks but causes rebar import to behave unexpectedly or fail validation downstream
findings.finding: The fallback payload spec requires a `ticket_type` field 'from the ticket content' but never defines the closed enum of valid values an executor must choose from; `common.schema.json#/$defs/ticket_type` enumerates ["bug", "epic", "story", "task", "session_log", "code_review", "identity"] but is never cited.
findings.location: Remediation — Fallback payload schema paragraph; AC6
scenarios: A ChatGPT session in fallback mode constructs a payload with ticket_type: 'feature' or 'chore' — not in the enum — which silently passes the plan's AC6 grep checks but is not a valid rebar ticket type
findings.suggested_fix: In the fallback payload schema paragraph, replace 'ticket_type from the ticket content' with 'ticket_type: one of ["bug", "epic", "story", "task", "session_log", "code_review", "identity"] (per src/rebar/schemas/common.schema.json#/$defs/ticket_type)'.

findings.finding: The plan mandates that the fallback payload must include a valid `ticket_type` (a required field per export.schema.json), but never enumerates the closed-set enum values a ChatGPT agent may legally supply. The actual enum — `["bug", "epic", "story", "task", "session_log", "code_review", "identity"]` — lives in `src/rebar/schemas/common.schema.json` but is not referenced or reproduced in the plan. An agent generating a fallback payload with no enum constraint could produce an invalid `ticket_type` value, causing silent skips or import failures.
findings.location: Remediation section — 'Fallback payload schema' subsection; AC6 (field list); AC8 invariant 5
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Fallback payload schema' subsection, add: 'ticket_type: one of `bug` | `epic` | `story` | `task` | `session_log` | `code_review` | `identity` (source: `src/rebar/schemas/common.schema.json`). ChatGPT sessions creating task/feature tickets should use `task`; work-decomposition containers use `story` or `epic`.' Update AC6's field-value list accordingly.

findings.finding: The capability-check step specifies `rebar exists <known-id>` to confirm tracker worktree validity, but the plan never defines what exit code or output to expect for each environmental failure case. Specifically: exit 0 = ticket found (tracker valid); exit 1 = ticket not found (ambiguous — could be wrong known-id OR empty store, not necessarily a missing tracker); non-0/non-1 = binary error or missing tracker. An agent following this instruction mechanically may classify a valid, empty tracker (or one where the 'known-id' is stale) as 'tracker-worktree fails', incorrectly triggering the fallback path.
findings.location: Remediation → Capability check and decision rule
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `rebar exists <known-id>` with `rebar list --status=open --limit=1` (or `rebar list` with a small limit) as the tracker-worktree probe, since a zero-result `list` with exit 0 definitively confirms tracker accessibility without depending on a specific ticket existing.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen AC1's proving command to anchor on the actual link path: replace `grep -qi 'chatgpt' AGENTS.md` with `grep -qi 'chatgpt-agent-guide' AGENTS.md` (or a regex that requires the file path, not just the word 'chatgpt').
evidence: AC1 states the criterion as 'discoverable from AGENTS.md without bloating the canonical startup context'
The proving command `grep -qi 'chatgpt' AGENTS.md` would pass if AGENTS.md contains a single sentence reading 'ChatGPT sessions should exercise caution' with no link
Per VERIFY-COMMAND DEFECT TAXONOMY item (1): pattern does not anchor to the actual link path
AC8 sub-invariant 1 correctly strengthens this to 'AGENTS.md links to docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md', but AC1's own stated proving command is still the weaker check
findings.finding: AC1's proving command `grep -qi 'chatgpt' AGENTS.md` is a substring false-positive: it passes whenever the word 'chatgpt' appears anywhere in AGENTS.md (e.g., in a comment, heading, or unrelated sentence) but does NOT verify that AGENTS.md actually contains a link to `docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md`, which is what 'discoverable from AGENTS.md' requires.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria – AC1: `grep -qi 'chatgpt' AGENTS.md`
scenarios: An executor adds a one-sentence mention of 'ChatGPT' to AGENTS.md without any link; AC1's grep passes and AC8 might not be run if AC1 is treated as sufficient standalone verification
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace the AC1 verify command with:
`grep -qiE 'chatgpt-agent-guide' AGENTS.md`
This ensures AGENTS.md contains the guide's filename (the link target), not merely the word 'chatgpt'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add one sentence of positive rationale in the Remediation section explaining why a standalone `docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md` is the right location (e.g., it is linked from `AGENTS.md` without adding startup-context weight, and it separates ChatGPT-specific connector-limited guidance from the general LLM framework docs).
evidence: Remediation paragraph 1 describes WHAT to add but never states WHY a new standalone doc rather than extending docs/llm-framework.md or docs/mcp-reference.md
AGENTS.md lines 6-12 establish the 'keep lean' + pointer philosophy, making the rationale inferable but not stated in the plan itself
AC1 says 'without bloating the canonical startup context' — this is stated as a constraint in a verify command, not as a design rationale in the Remediation body
findings.finding: The plan's Remediation section never states a positive rationale for choosing a standalone `docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md` document over expanding an existing doc (e.g., `docs/llm-framework.md`). The AGENTS.md 'keep lean' philosophy is referenced only indirectly in AC1's verify command context, not as an explicit design statement in the Remediation.
findings.location: Remediation section (first paragraph and AC1)
scenarios: An implementer choosing where to place the content may not understand that doc/llm-framework.md (the existing LLM agent operations pointer) was considered and rejected, and may merge the content there instead, defeating the discoverability goal.

findings.finding: The capability-check step instructs the agent to confirm tracker worktree validity via `rebar exists <known-id>` but does not specify what the agent should do when that command itself returns an unexpected result — a non-boolean error, a timeout, an unexpected exit code, or an exception — rather than a clean true/false. This leaves the decision rule undefined for an ambiguous capability-check outcome, which is a plausible non-happy-path in connector-limited or partially degraded environments.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC2; Remediation section: 'tracker-worktree dimension … confirmed via `rebar exists <known-id>` or equivalent'
findings.suggested_fix: State that any unexpected or non-boolean result from the tracker-worktree confirmation step should be treated as 'dimension fails' (conservative fallback), and that the agent must report the raw error output to the operator as part of the capability-gap report so the ambiguity is transparent rather than silently resolved.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit deliverable entry in the Remediation section (or a new 'Deliverables' list) naming `tests/unit/test_chatgpt_guide_links.py` and summarizing its five invariants, so the test file is a described work item with AC8 as its covering criterion.
evidence: Remediation says: 'Add concise ChatGPT-specific guidance … Define a safe fallback … Document the exceptional import path' — no mention of a test file.
AC8 says: 'A lightweight documentation test (`tests/unit/test_chatgpt_guide_links.py`) asserts each of the following specific invariants … and exits 0 only if ALL hold'
The Remediation section contains no work item or deliverable that corresponds to creating, scoping, or maintaining this test file.
findings.finding: AC8 specifies creating a test file (`tests/unit/test_chatgpt_guide_links.py`) with five named invariants, but the Remediation section's described work contains no corresponding deliverable — the Remediation lists only documentation additions (a guidance file, AGENTS.md link, fallback schema, import mechanism description). This is an orphan criterion: AC8 measures a deliverable that is never described or scoped in the plan body.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC8 vs. Remediation section
scenarios: An implementer reading the Remediation section would not know to write the test file; an implementer reading AC8 would be surprised this work was not described.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Remediation section, after the final 'Cascade flag' paragraph, add: 'Also add `tests/unit/test_chatgpt_guide_links.py` — a lightweight documentation test that asserts the five invariants enumerated in AC8 against `docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md` and `AGENTS.md`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a pytest fixture or unit-test probe (e.g., in `tests/unit/test_import_ndjson_dryrun.py`) that runs `import_ndjson` in dry-run mode against a minimal NDJSON file containing one record missing `ticket_id` and one valid record, and asserts the specific counter/warning behavior the guide documents (no warning, no counter increment for the malformed record; `would_create==1`, `skipped==0`) — confirming the detection algorithm before encoding it in the guide.
evidence: Plan states: 'in dry-run mode (~lines 144-161), a record missing ticket_id/ticket_type hits a bare continue with no counter increment and no warning at all (the warnings list is never populated in the dry-run branch)'
Plan states: 'only already-imported records (a source_id already present in the target) increment skipped'

Plan derives detection rule: 'any shortfall is exactly the count of malformed (missing ticket_id/ticket_type) records... detectable by comparing would_create + skipped against the total NDJSON line/record count'
AC8 test only checks documentation phrase presence, not actual import behavior
No spike, fixture, or probe against real `rebar import --dry-run` execution is planned
findings.finding: The plan encodes specific, non-obvious internal code behavior from `src/rebar/_io/import_ndjson.py` (dry-run branch produces NO warning and NO counter increment for missing-ticket_id/ticket_type records; live import produces a warning but no counter increment; `skipped` counts only already-imported records) as operator-facing guidance and derives a detection algorithm (`would_create + skipped == total record count`) from those assertions — but no empirical validation step (fixture, probe, or RED test) is planned to verify these behavioral claims before the guide is written.
findings.location: Remediation section / AC7 — `docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md` dry-run behavior and detection algorithm; `src/rebar/_io/import_ndjson.py` lines 144-161, 205, 221-227
scenarios: If the dry-run branch has been updated to emit warnings or increment a counter since the code was inspected, the detection algorithm in the guide will give operators false confidence that a `would_create + skipped == total` match means no malformed records

If the live-import warning behavior differs from what is described (e.g., warnings are emitted to stderr not captured in a warnings list), operators following the guide will misdiagnose import problems
A maintainer writing the guide based on these assertions without running the code may propagate any reading error into permanent operator instructions
findings.suggested_fix: Add a small test (or extend an existing import test) that calls the import function directly in dry-run mode with: (1) one well-formed record, (2) one record missing `ticket_id`, (3) one already-imported record. Assert `result.would_create == 1`, `result.skipped == 1`, `len(result.warnings) == 0`, and that `would_create + skipped (2) < total_lines (3)` — confirming the shortfall-detection algorithm. Reference this test in the plan's remediation section so guide authors can verify their prose matches the tested behavior.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a lightweight test (separate from the prose-grep tests in AC8) that actually invokes `rebar import --dry-run` (or directly calls `import_ndjson` in dry-run mode) with a fixture containing a record missing `ticket_id`, and asserts the resulting counters and warnings list, so the guide's behavioral claims are anchored to live code rather than only to guide prose.
evidence: Plan states: 'in dry-run mode...a record missing ticket_id/ticket_type hits a bare continue with no counter increment and no warning at all'
Plan states AC8 test 'exits 0 only if ALL hold' but test items 1-5 only check guide prose and AGENTS.md links, not actual import_ndjson.py behavior
AC8 item 5 reads the live schema file (good), but no AC8 item reads import_ndjson.py to validate the dry-run behavioral claims remain accurate
ABSENCE: no plan element (e.g., a test that imports a malformed NDJSON and asserts counter/warning behavior) anchors the guide's dry-run prose to the live implementation
findings.finding: The guidance document will embed specific behavioral assertions about import_ndjson.py's dry-run internals (no counter increment, no warning for malformed records in dry-run mode) that are tied to specific implementation lines. The unit test (AC8) validates only the guide's prose via grep, not the actual code behavior. If import_ndjson.py is refactored (e.g., a future fix adds a warning or counter increment for malformed records in dry-run), the guide becomes incorrect while the test still passes — silently mis-specifying agent behavior.
findings.location: AC7 verify clause / Remediation 'Exceptional import mechanism' / tests/unit/test_chatgpt_guide_links.py (AC8)
scenarios: import_ndjson.py dry-run branch is patched to emit a warning for malformed records; guide still says 'no warning' and test still passes — agents following the guide undercount malformed records
A refactor renames or restructures the dry-run path; guide's 'comparing would_create + skipped == total record count' recipe silently breaks
findings.suggested_fix: In `tests/unit/test_chatgpt_guide_links.py` (or a companion file), add a test that calls `from rebar._io.import_ndjson import import_ndjson` with `dry_run=True` and a fixture NDJSON line lacking `ticket_id`, then asserts `result.would_create == 0`, `result.skipped == 0`, and `result.warnings == []` (or whatever the actual return structure is). This makes AC7's behavioral claims self-verifying and alerts maintainers when the implementation changes.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a sentence to the Remediation or Finding section acknowledging the two existing provider-specific guidance patterns (CLAUDE.md thin bridge; .agents/rules/rebar.md summary) and stating why a standalone `docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md` is preferred (e.g., the content is substantive enough to warrant its own doc, not a harness bridge file).
evidence: CLAUDE.md: '@AGENTS.md' one-line import — the established thin-bridge pattern for Claude Code
.agents/rules/rebar.md: 'Google Antigravity's .agents/rules/* cannot @-import another file, so this is a short reference-and-summary' — the established summary pattern for harnesses without @-import
AGENTS.md header: 'Keep this file lean: an @-import loads at launch and does not reduce context, so already-documented topics are one-line pointers into docs/'
Plan Remediation: 'Add concise ChatGPT-specific guidance' — no comparison to or justification over the two existing patterns
findings.finding: The plan proposes a new docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md file without acknowledging or comparing against two established provider-specific guidance patterns already in the repo (CLAUDE.md thin @-import bridge; .agents/rules/rebar.md summary-and-pointer for harnesses that can't @-import). The plan does not explain why a substantive docs/ file is preferred over extending the canonical AGENTS.md with a connector-limited section (which all harnesses would inherit) or adding a thin CHATGPT.md bridge file. Without this justification the chosen pattern may diverge unnecessarily from the established approach.
findings.location: Remediation section / AC1 — 'A ChatGPT-specific guidance file or clearly labeled section is discoverable from AGENTS.md'
scenarios: A future maintainer adds a third provider-specific pattern that duplicates the existing CLAUDE.md pattern, increasing maintenance surface unnecessarily.
Connector-limited operation guidance lives in docs/ but is invisible to harnesses that load only AGENTS.md without following the link, defeating the intent of a lean canonical context.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a failure-exit specification to the exceptional import guidance (AC7 / Remediation) that instructs operators what to do if `rebar import` returns a runtime error (e.g., lock contention or git push rejection): at minimum, state whether to retry, how to confirm no partial write occurred, and when to escalate.
evidence: AC7 specifies: 'requires confirming the target branch head before invocation, and requires running `rebar validate` or `rebar fsck` afterward' — but no guidance for the failure case between those two steps.

Cascade flag: 'guidance must remain consistent with Rebar's append-only, optimistic-concurrency, and native-command invariants' — optimistic concurrency implies the possibility of a conflict/retry cycle, but no retry or error-recovery instruction is specified.
The plan covers pre-import (dry-run, line-count comparison) and post-import (validate/fsck) steps but leaves a gap for the in-flight failure state.
findings.finding: The guidance spec defines a pre-import validation step (dry-run + line-count check) and a post-import integrity step (rebar validate / rebar fsck), but specifies no guidance for the error-exit when `rebar import` itself fails at runtime — e.g., lock acquisition failure, git push rejection on the tickets branch, or HLC timestamp conflict. An operator who follows the documented flow and receives a runtime import error has no remediation path specified. The cascade flag references optimistic-concurrency invariants, implying conflicts are possible, yet the guidance spec is silent on what to do when one occurs.
findings.location: Remediation — 'Exceptional import mechanism' paragraph and AC7
scenarios: An operator runs `rebar import` and receives a lock-contention or git-push-rejection error; the guidance gives no instruction on whether to retry, inspect partial state, or escalate — the operator is left without a documented next step.
findings.suggested_fix: In the guidance spec for the import flow, add a brief error-exit clause: 'If `rebar import` exits non-zero, do not retry immediately — run `rebar validate` (or `rebar fsck`) first to confirm no partial event was written, then resolve any lock or remote-head conflict (e.g., `git fetch` + rebase the tickets branch) before re-running the import. The NDJSON payload is idempotent by `source_id`, so a safe retry after conflict resolution will not create duplicates.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit co-location requirement to AC6 (or a new AC): the decision rule trigger condition and the fallback payload schema MUST appear in the same numbered section or one must directly reference the other by section heading, not merely co-exist in the same file.
evidence: Plan defines the 6-dimension decision rule in one paragraph: 'Capability check and decision rule (resolves T8 findings)'
Plan defines the fallback payload schema in a separate paragraph: 'Fallback payload schema (resolves T8 finding on AC6)'

AC6 verify: 'grep -qi export.schema.json docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md && grep -qi schema_version docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md' — passes if both strings appear anywhere in the file, even in unrelated sections
No AC or plan requirement states the decision rule and the payload schema must appear in the same section or reference each other directly
findings.finding: The capability-check decision rule (6-dimension check, precise trigger condition) and the fallback payload schema are described in separate prose paragraphs with no co-location requirement in the guidance file; the AC verify commands check for keyword presence independently, creating a pink-elephant risk where an implementation satisfies both greps but places the decision rule and the payload schema in disconnected sections, severing the processing-protocol-co-located-with-schema guarantee.
findings.location: Remediation — Capability check paragraph vs. Fallback payload schema paragraph; AC6 verify command
scenarios: An implementer places the 6-dimension capability check in section 2 and the fallback payload schema in section 5; both grep checks pass, but an executor reading section 2 does not know what to produce when the trigger fires, and reading section 5 does not know when to use it
findings.suggested_fix: Amend AC6's verify command to also check for a section anchor, e.g., require that 'impossible' (the trigger word) and 'schema_version' appear within N lines of each other: grep -n 'impossible\|schema_version' docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md | awk -F: 'NR>1{if ($1 - prev < 20) found=1} {prev=$1} END{exit !found}'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the unverified `rebar exists <known-id>` confirmation step with a documented, verifiable command (e.g., `rebar show <known-id> --output json` or `rebar list --status=open --limit=1`) or tool-verify that `rebar exists` is a real CLI subcommand before publishing the guidance.
evidence: Plan text: 'tracker worktree (a directory containing a valid tickets branch checkout, confirmed via rebar exists <known-id> or equivalent)'
AGENTS.md library quick reference and docs/mcp-reference.md tool table have no 'exists' command listed
The 'or equivalent' escape hatch is present but provides no concrete alternative, leaving the confirmation mechanism unspecified for implementations that cannot verify the command exists
findings.finding: The plan cites `rebar exists <known-id>` as the operationalization of the tracker-worktree capability check, but this command is not documented in AGENTS.md, docs/mcp-reference.md, or any referenced docs/ file — its existence and behavior are unverified by the plan, leaving the decision rule's confirmation step undefined.
findings.location: Remediation — Capability check paragraph ('confirmed via rebar exists <known-id> or equivalent')
scenarios: A ChatGPT session following the guide runs 'rebar exists <id>' to confirm tracker access; the command does not exist, returns an unexpected error or usage message, and the session incorrectly concludes the tracker worktree is inaccessible

findings.finding: The plan's Evidence section asserts that in live-run mode, a record missing `ticket_id`/`ticket_type` produces 'an incremented `skipped` count in the return summary' — but reading `import_ndjson.py` lines 205, 221-228, and 383-390 shows that the `skipped` counter is only incremented at line 227 for `sid in seen_source` (already-imported-by-source_id records). Missing-field records hit `warn()` + `continue` at lines 223-225 with NO `skipped` increment; they are invisible to `skipped` and appear only in the `warnings` list. The plan's behavioral claim is factually wrong, and downstream AC7 guidance (the `would_create + skipped == total` detection heuristic) partially rests on this premise.
findings.location: Evidence section: '`src/rebar/schemas/export.schema.json`'s `required` array...' paragraph; specifically the clause 'an incremented `skipped` count in the return summary'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'an incremented `skipped` count in the return summary' with 'a `warnings` list entry only — the `skipped` counter is NOT incremented for malformed records in either mode; `skipped` exclusively counts already-imported (source_id-present) deduplication hits.'

findings.finding: The plan's Evidence section cites `src/rebar/_io/import_ndjson.py (~line 92-95)` as the location of the warn+skip behavior for missing `ticket_id`/`ticket_type`, but reading those lines shows they contain `_scan_existing_source_ids()` — the function that reads already-imported source_ids from the store — not the record-validation/warn path. The actual validation code is at lines 221-225 (inside the Pass 1 loop). The wrong line citation means an implementer directed to read the cited location will find unrelated code.
findings.location: Evidence section: '`src/rebar/_io/import_ndjson.py` (~line 92-95) checks only `ticket_id`/`ticket_type` directly'
findings.suggested_fix: Change '`src/rebar/_io/import_ndjson.py` (~line 92-95)' to '`src/rebar/_io/import_ndjson.py` (~lines 221-225, inside the Pass 1 loop)' in the Evidence section.

findings.finding: The plan's Evidence section cites `src/rebar/_io/import_ndjson.py` ~line 92-95 as the location of the 'silently skips the record with a logged warning' behavior for missing `ticket_id`/`ticket_type`, but lines 92-95 of the actual file contain `_scan_existing_source_ids` (which maps `source_id → local_id`). The actual warn-and-skip for missing `ticket_id`/`ticket_type` in live mode is at line 224. This misattribution is material because the plan quotes these line numbers as ground truth for the behavioral claim that `rebar import` does not raise and does not check `schema_version`/`status`/`title`.
findings.location: Evidence section: '`src/rebar/_io/import_ndjson.py` (~line 92-95) checks only `ticket_id`/`ticket_type`'
findings.suggested_fix: Update Evidence to: '`src/rebar/_io/import_ndjson.py` (line 224, live mode) warns and skips records missing `ticket_id`/`ticket_type`; lines 149-151 (dry-run branch) use a bare `continue` with no warning and no counter increment.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
